quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Indefinite integral"?

options.text: [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
Take ln of both sides, differentiate implicitly, solve for dy/dx; essential when both base and exponent are variable
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C; represents the family of all antiderivatives, differing by a constant C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Indefinite integral" is defined as: ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C; represents the family of all antiderivatives, differing by a constant C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Constant of integration"?

options.text: Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b
F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1
f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Constant of integration" is defined as: The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Power rule for integration"?

options.text: If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C for n ≠ -1; reverses the power rule for derivatives

quiz_questions.explanation: "Power rule for integration" is defined as: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C for n ≠ -1; reverses the power rule for derivatives. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Initial value problem"?

options.text: The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs
Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Initial value problem" is defined as: Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals, which best defines "Antiderivative"?

options.text: A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the reverse operation of differentiation
Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: In Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals, "Antiderivative" refers to: A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the reverse operation of differentiation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C; represents the family of all antiderivatives, differing by a constant C"?

options.text: Table-based accumulation
Free response strategy
Indefinite integral
Linearity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Indefinite integral".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C"?

options.text: Position, velocity, acceleration
Constant of integration
Justification language
Limit does not exist

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Constant of integration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C for n ≠ -1; reverses the power rule for derivatives"?

options.text: Power rule for integration
Direct substitution
Common quotient rule errors
Units of derivative

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Power rule for integration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C"?

options.text: Initial value problem
Domain restrictions
Sign chart of f''
Verifying a solution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Initial value problem".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals, which term matches: "A function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x); the reverse operation of differentiation"?

options.text: Antiderivative
Fractional exponent derivatives
Shadow problems
Left/Right/Midpoint sum

quiz_questions.explanation: In Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals, this refers to "Antiderivative".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals is accurate?

options.text: Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.
Derivatives represent rates of change in any context: population growth rates, velocity, temperature change rates, marginal economic quantities, and reaction rates.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.
Then solve algebraically for dy/dx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.
An antiderivative of f(x) is a function F(x) such that F'(x) = f(x).
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Drug dosing models use DEs to track drug concentration in the bloodstream.
The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration (since derivatives of constants are zero).
If f'(t) is the rate of water flowing into a tank, then ∫₀ᵀ f'(t)dt is the net change in water volume.
This technique is essential for functions like xˣ, (sin x)^x, or x^(1/x) where both base and exponent are variable.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Differential Equation Basics introduces equations involving derivatives.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.
The derivative of an inverse function is (f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)), derived from the chain rule applied to f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.
Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.
Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Physics: integrating acceleration a(t) gives velocity v(t) = ∫a(t)dt + v₀, and integrating velocity gives position s(t) = ∫v(t)dt + s₀
Understanding S-curves in product adoption: inflection point marks the transition from rapid early adoption growth to market saturation deceleration
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Population modeling: if the growth rate dP/dt = f(t) is known, integrating gives the population P(t) = ∫f(t)dt + P₀
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Bacterial growth: E. coli doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions; 1 bacterium becomes ~70 billion in 12 hours (2³⁶ ≈ 6.9 × 10¹⁰)
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Engineering: integrating a force-displacement curve to find work done W = ∫F(x)dx, or integrating current to find total charge Q = ∫I(t)dt
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals?

options.text: Physics: integrating acceleration a(t) gives velocity v(t) = ∫a(t)dt + v₀, and integrating velocity gives position s(t) = ∫v(t)dt + s₀
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Cooling/heating: Newton's law of cooling dT/dt = -k(T-Tₛ) is separable, giving T(t) = Tₛ + (T₀-Tₛ)e^(-kt)
Average voltage of AC power: V_avg = (1/T)∫₀ᵀ V(t)dt; for sinusoidal AC, the average over a full cycle is zero, motivating the use of RMS voltage

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals in practice?

options.text: Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment
Population modeling: if the growth rate dP/dt = f(t) is known, integrating gives the population P(t) = ∫f(t)dt + P₀
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections
Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals.
